articles.title: Pope Leo XIV's First Encyclical Calls for AI to Be Disarmed
articles.summary: Pope Leo XIV released his first encyclical, Magnifica Humanitas, on May 25, 2026, a sweeping 42,300-word document warning that artificial intelligence risks creating "new digital slaveries" and becoming a tool of domination without urgent moral and legal limits. The document, titled "Magnificent Humanity," calls on governments, tech companies and individuals to "disarm" AI by removing it from unchecked military and economic competition. It is the first major teaching document of Leo's papacy and is already being compared to landmark encyclicals of previous centuries.
articles.content:
Pope Leo XIV did not ease into his papacy gently. On May 25, 2026, the Vatican released Magnifica Humanitas, the first encyclical of Leo's pontificate, and it is a document with a lot to say. All 42,300 words of it are aimed squarely at artificial intelligence and what unchecked AI development means for human dignity, democracy and the future of work.

An encyclical is a formal letter from the Pope to Catholic bishops and, increasingly in modern times, to the whole world. This one, whose full Latin title translates to "Magnificent Humanity," frames AI as a second industrial revolution. Pope Leo XIV chose to sign the document on May 15, 135 years to the day since his namesake Pope Leo XIII signed Rerum Novarum, the landmark 1891 encyclical on workers' rights during the first industrial revolution. The message is deliberate: the Catholic Church sees AI as the defining moral test of this era, just as industrialisation was for the last.

The central demand is bold. Magnifica Humanitas calls for AI to be "disarmed," which Leo defines as freeing AI from the logic of armed competition in both military and economic contexts. He warns that AI in the hands of a powerful few risks becoming a new form of colonialism, with personal data described as "the new rare earths of power." The document calls for robust legal frameworks, independent oversight and for individuals to have the right to control their own data. It also takes a firm stance on AI in warfare, arguing that the traditional "just war" theory is now outdated and that military force can only be justified for self-defence in the strictest sense.

In a remarkable move, Pope Leo XIV personally presented Magnifica Humanitas to the world at the Vatican alongside Chris Olah, a co-founder of AI research company Anthropic. Past popes have typically handed the role of presenting an encyclical to cardinals or other senior figures. Leo's decision to stand beside an AI company founder signals that he wants a genuine dialogue with the tech world, not just a lecture at it.

The document also raises urgent concerns about what AI means for young people and for education, calling for a complete rethink of how society prepares people to live and work alongside these systems. Whether governments and tech giants will listen remains to be seen. But with 1.4 billion Catholics worldwide and the moral authority of the papacy behind it, Magnifica Humanitas is not a document that will be easy to ignore.
keyPoints: Pope Leo XIV released his first encyclical, Magnifica Humanitas, on May 25, 2026
The 42,300-word document calls for AI to be "disarmed" and freed from unchecked military and economic competition
Leo warns that AI controlled by a powerful few represents a new form of colonialism over personal data
The encyclical was presented alongside Anthropic co-founder Chris Olah, a first for any papal document
Leo signed the document on May 15, exactly 135 years after Pope Leo XIII signed the workers' rights encyclical Rerum Novarum

articles.title: Knicks Reach NBA Finals for First Time Since 1999
articles.summary: The New York Knicks punched their ticket to the NBA Finals on Monday night with a dominant 130-93 rout of the Cleveland Cavaliers in Game 4, completing a four-game sweep of the Eastern Conference Finals. It is the Knicks' first Finals appearance in 27 years, since the 1999 season. Karl Anthony-Towns led the way with 19 points and 14 rebounds as New York never let Cleveland get comfortable.
articles.content:
Madison Square Garden is going to be electric. The New York Knicks are heading to the NBA Finals for the first time since 1999, and they did it in style. On Monday night, the Knicks crushed the Cleveland Cavaliers 130-93 in Game 4 to complete a clean four-game sweep of the Eastern Conference Finals.

Karl Anthony-Towns was enormous all series and continued that form in the clincher, finishing with 19 points and 14 rebounds. OG Anunoby added 17 points as New York's depth proved too much for Cleveland to handle. It was a statement performance from a team that has looked focused and relentless throughout the postseason. The Cavaliers, who featured Donovan Mitchell, James Harden and Evan Mobley, simply could not find an answer.

The road to this moment was not always smooth. In Game 1 of this series, Jalen Brunson sparked one of the most remarkable comebacks in recent postseason memory, rallying the Knicks from a 22-point deficit in the fourth quarter to win 115-104 in overtime, finishing with 38 points. That win seemed to break the Cavaliers' spirit entirely. By the time Game 4 came around, Cleveland had no fight left.

For Knicks fans who have waited 27 long years for this, it is hard to overstate what this means. The last time New York reached the Finals was during the lockout-shortened 1999 season, when they fell to the San Antonio Spurs. A whole generation of fans has grown up never seeing their team on the game's biggest stage. That wait is now over. Game 1 of the NBA Finals tips off on June 3 on ABC.
keyPoints: The New York Knicks swept the Cleveland Cavaliers 4-0 to reach the NBA Finals
The Knicks' 130-93 Game 4 win was the most dominant performance of the series
Karl Anthony-Towns posted 19 points and 14 rebounds in the clinching game
This is New York's first NBA Finals appearance since 1999, a gap of 27 years
NBA Finals Game 1 is scheduled for June 3 on ABC
articles.wikiSearchQuery: New York Knicks NBA basketball team

articles.title: Ferrari Luce Is Here: Jony Ive Designed Every Inch of It
articles.summary: Ferrari has officially unveiled the Ferrari Luce, its first ever electric vehicle, designed in full collaboration with Jony Ive and Marc Newson at their creative studio LoveFrom. The Luce is a four-door, five-seat grand tourer powered by four electric motors producing 1,035 horsepower, with a starting price of €550,000. It marks the most dramatic reinvention in Ferrari's history and the most ambitious car project LoveFrom has ever taken on.
articles.content:
Ferrari has done something it once swore it would never do. The Ferrari Luce, the Italian brand's first fully electric car, was unveiled yesterday in Rome, and it is unlike any Ferrari that has come before it. The name means "light" in Italian, and the design philosophy matches. Ferrari launched the Luce as a large sports car designed by Jony Ive and Marc Newson's studio LoveFrom. This is not a small tweak to a familiar silhouette. This is a full reboot.

The four-door, five-seat Luce is powered by four electric motors providing up to 1,035 horsepower, and it is equipped with a high-capacity 122 kWh battery. Ferrari claims the car can travel up to 530 kilometres on a single charge. Performance is ferocious too. The car does 0 to 100 km/h in 2.5 seconds, 0 to 200 km/h in 6.8 seconds, and hits a top speed of 310 km/h. And yet somehow the biggest story is not the numbers. It is the design.

The interior was designed by LoveFrom, with an emphasis on physical controls over touchscreens and a cockpit shaped around glass and aluminium. Jony Ive's philosophy here is direct. Ive argues that touchscreens made sense for the iPhone because it solved a general-purpose problem, but not for driving: "To use touch in a car is something I would never dream of doing, because it requires that you look at what you're doing." The instrument cluster in the cockpit is digital, but the display is comprised of two OLED panels layered on top of each other with physical needles sandwiched in between. It is classic Ive: analogue warmth wrapped in precision engineering.

The Luce is the result of a close collaboration over five years between LoveFrom and Ferrari's in-house design and engineering teams. Ferrari developed all the main components at its base in Maranello, resulting in over 60 new patents. Luce pricing starts at €550,000 ($640,000) in Europe with production set to begin in late 2026, and the car will launch in the US in the second quarter of 2027. It will be the most expensive Ferrari ever made. Whether the internet agrees it looks the part is, as you are about to read, a whole other story.
keyPoints: The Ferrari Luce is Ferrari's first ever fully electric car, unveiled in Rome on May 25, 2026
It was designed entirely by Jony Ive and Marc Newson at LoveFrom over five years
Four electric motors produce 1,035 horsepower and the 122 kWh battery gives up to 530 km of range
The interior deliberately avoids touchscreens in favour of tactile physical controls made from glass and anodised aluminium
Pricing starts at €550,000 in Europe and US deliveries begin in Q2 2027

articles.title: ClickUp Fired 22% of Staff and Replaced Them With 3,000 AI Agents
articles.summary: ClickUp CEO Zeb Evans cut 290 jobs — 22% of the company's workforce — and replaced them with roughly 3,000 internal AI agents, framing the move not as cost-cutting but as a structural bet on AI that he calls the "100x org." Surviving employees can now earn up to $1 million a year in cash if they prove they can generate outsized output using those agents. The move is one of the clearest and most blunt examples yet of AI directly replacing human roles at a major tech company.
articles.content:
Most tech CEOs who do mass layoffs reach for soft language about "strategic realignment" and "investing in the future." ClickUp CEO Zeb Evans did not bother with the euphemisms.

Zeb Evans, CEO of the collaboration software startup ClickUp, announced on X that the company, which was last valued in 2021 at $4 billion, had laid off 22% of its workforce, yet characterized that reduction not as a cost-cutting measure, but rather as a radical embrace of AI that will propel the company to the next level. In real numbers, ClickUp cut 290 jobs on May 21, 2026, roughly 22% of its 1,300-person workforce. Those are real people with real jobs gone, and Evans is being unusually direct about why.

ClickUp recently introduced roughly 3,000 internal AI agents to handle a wide range of complex tasks on behalf of its employees. Instead of performing the work themselves, staff members are now expected to direct these agents and ultimately review the output to ensure it meets the company's standards. That means ClickUp now runs a 3:1 ratio of AI agents to human employees. Evans had already mandated that staff go through an AI agent trained to stand in his place before contacting him directly. So yes, even getting time with the CEO now requires going through an AI first.

For the employees who survived the cuts, there is a serious financial upside on the table. ClickUp is introducing salary bands that reach $1 million per year in cash. The path is available to nearly anyone in the company who produces "100x impact" by creating or managing AI systems. Evans frames this as the company's new operating model, which he calls the "100x org." His argument is simple: if one person can now do the work of a hundred with the help of AI agents, that person deserves to be paid accordingly.

But is this actually working? According to a recent Gartner survey, about 80% of companies using autonomous tech have cut jobs. However, the study found that workforce reductions are not necessarily translating into meaningful financial returns. Evans told TechCrunch that ClickUp is genuinely seeing productivity gains and plans to bake those efficiencies into a new product for customers. He also pushed back against the growing trend of measuring AI adoption by token usage, saying "Instead of gamifying token cost, we gamify value created and time saved."

ClickUp is not operating in isolation. The US tech industry has shed more than 100,000 jobs across roughly 250 layoff events in 2026 so far. Meta cut 8,000 roles the same week despite posting record revenue. Oracle eliminated up to 30,000 positions to fund AI infrastructure buildout. What makes ClickUp's move stand out is the raw honesty of it. ClickUp is unusual only in how directly Evans states the logic. Most CEOs reach for efficiency language. Evans is explicitly calling the eliminated roles structurally obsolete. Whether that honesty is admirable or alarming depends entirely on which side of the layoff you are on.
keyPoints: ClickUp CEO Zeb Evans cut 290 jobs — 22% of staff — on May 21, 2026 and replaced them with 3,000 AI agents
ClickUp now runs 3,000 internal AI agents, a 3:1 ratio of agents to human employees
Surviving employees can earn up to $1 million per year in cash if they generate "100x impact" with AI
A Gartner survey found 80% of companies using autonomous tech have cut jobs, but returns are not consistent
ClickUp, valued at $4 billion, reported around $300 million in annual recurring revenue in 2025

articles.title: McCartney and His Future Self Paul Mescal Talk The Boys of Dungeon Lane
articles.summary: Paul McCartney sat down with Paul Mescal, the actor who will play him in Sam Mendes' upcoming Beatles biopics, for an intimate short film streaming now on Amazon Live and the Amazon Music app. Titled The Boys of Dungeon Lane: In Conversation with Paul McCartney and Paul Mescal, the 10-minute film digs into the stories behind five tracks on McCartney's 27th studio album, which drops May 29. It is one of the most unusual album rollouts in recent memory: the man and the actor who will embody his legacy, sitting down together before the world has even heard the record.
articles.content:
It is not often that an artist gets to be interviewed by the person who will play them in a movie. But that is exactly what happened when Paul McCartney sat down with Paul Mescal for a short film now streaming on Amazon. The Boys of Dungeon Lane: In Conversation with Paul McCartney and Paul Mescal premiered on Amazon Live and the Amazon Music app. Mescal will play McCartney in director Sam Mendes' biopics, The Beatles: A Four-Film Cinematic Event, scheduled for release in 2027. So this is not just a promo chat. It is a meeting between a legend and the actor tasked with bringing his early life to the big screen.

In the 10-minute chat, filmed in the same restaurant where the album trailer was shot, McCartney and Mescal talked about the nostalgic The Boys of Dungeon Lane. The conversation is warm and funny from the jump. When Mescal opened by asking how McCartney feels about being interviewed, McCartney replied: "It depends if I like the person. Which is where we're running into a problem already." He then added: "No, I find if I like who I'm being interviewed by, it comes easy."

Over the course of the conversation, The Boys of Dungeon Lane tracks "Down South", "Home to Us", "Lost Horizon", "Salesman Saint", and "Days We Left Behind" are brought to life via revelations about McCartney's songwriting, early memories of his parents and bandmates and more. One of the most moving moments comes when Mescal brings up "Days We Left Behind", the album's first single, which touches on McCartney's bond with John Lennon. "Looking back on your life, I ran into this guy called John Lennon, and he was fighting life," McCartney said. "When it came to writing, that kind of relationship stayed there, so on this record, I might even refer to him in my mind, as if we're still writing together."

Set for release May 29 via MPL/Capitol Records, The Boys of Dungeon Lane features the first single "Days We Left Behind" and McCartney's first ever duet with Ringo Starr, "Home to Us". The conversation flows like a trip down memory lane, with McCartney talking about the songwriting behind his 27th album as well as memories surrounding his early life, parents, and bandmates. The album is arguably his most personal and introspective to date, with untold stories that transport the listener back to where it all began, as McCartney writes with rare openness about his childhood in post-war Liverpool, the resilience of his parents, and early adventures with George Harrison and John Lennon long before the world had ever heard of Beatlemania.
keyPoints: Paul McCartney and Paul Mescal's short film The Boys of Dungeon Lane: In Conversation is streaming now on Amazon Live and the Amazon Music app
Mescal is set to play McCartney in Sam Mendes' The Beatles: A Four-Film Cinematic Event
The film covers five tracks from the new album including the first ever McCartney and Ringo Starr duet, Home to Us
The Boys of Dungeon Lane is McCartney's 27th studio album and drops May 29 via MPL/Capitol Records
McCartney reveals he still writes with Lennon in his mind, as if they are collaborating together
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Paul McCartney The Boys of Dungeon Lane album

articles.title: Lenny Kravitz Is the Villain in 007 First Light, Out Tomorrow
articles.summary: IO Interactive's 007 First Light drops May 27 on PS5, Xbox Series X/S, and PC, and the launch trailer has just confirmed that Lenny Kravitz voices the game's main antagonist, a character named Bawma. Irish actor Patrick Gibson plays a 26-year-old James Bond in this standalone origin story developed with Amazon MGM Studios. The game marks the first major Bond video game in over a decade, and Lana Del Rey and composer David Arnold wrote an original title sequence song for it.
articles.content:
Tomorrow is a big day for Bond fans. IO Interactive's 007 First Light hits PS5, Xbox Series X/S, and PC on May 27, and the launch trailer just dropped one final surprise: rock legend Lenny Kravitz is voicing the game's villain, a character named Bawma.

007 First Light follows a 26-year-old James Bond as a recruit in the MI6 training program, with Irish actor Patrick Gibson cast as Bond. Gibson said he was "pretty stunned" after learning he had been cast, and narrative director Martin Emborg noted that Gibson had a level of "built-in impatience" that was "perfect" for a young James Bond.

Gibson is joined by Lennie James as Greenway, Priyanga Burford as M, Alastair Mackenzie as Q, Kiera Lester as Moneypenny, and Noémie Nakai as Ms. Roth. Gemma Chan also appears as Dr. Selina Tan, an original character created by IO Interactive. And then there is Kravitz, whose villain Bawma was announced at The Game Awards 2025 in December.

Popular American singer Lana Del Rey and longtime Bond composer David Arnold have written and composed an original song for the game's title sequence. Bond games had been on ice since publisher Activision had its licence revoked after 007 Legends in 2012, before IO Interactive announced development of a new Bond game in November 2020. 007 First Light is scheduled to release for PlayStation 5, Windows, and Xbox Series X/S on 27 May 2026, with a Nintendo Switch 2 version to follow later in the year.
keyPoints: 007 First Light launches May 27 on PS5, Xbox Series X/S, and PC
Lenny Kravitz voices the villain Bawma in his first major video game role
Patrick Gibson plays a 26-year-old James Bond in this standalone origin story
Lana Del Rey and David Arnold created the original title sequence song
Nintendo Switch 2 version is coming in Summer 2026

articles.title: BTS Win Artist of the Year at 2026 AMAs: 'We Made It Once Again'
articles.summary: BTS swept the 2026 American Music Awards on Monday night in Las Vegas, winning Artist of the Year, Song of the Summer for their Hot 100 number one hit "SWIM", and Best Male K-Pop Artist. It was the group's first appearance at an awards show in four years, and it brought the house down at the MGM Grand Garden Arena. The triple win pushes BTS to 14 career AMA trophies.
articles.content:
BTS are back and they brought receipts. The seven-member group made a triumphant return to the American Music Awards stage on Monday night after a four-year absence, and they left with three trophies. BTS once again proved their global influence by winning Artist of the Year, one of the top honours at the American Music Awards, for the second time at the ceremony held in Las Vegas on May 25th. Their acceptance cry of "we made it once again" summed up the moment perfectly.

For anyone catching up: BTS took a two-year hiatus as the members fulfilled South Korean military service requirements. "Hooligan" appears on BTS's Billboard 200-topping album ARIRANG, which dropped earlier this year after that two-year hiatus to fulfil military service requirements and pursue solo projects. The comeback has been enormous. The track "Hooligan" has peaked at No. 35 on the Billboard Hot 100, while ARIRANG lead single "SWIM" topped that chart in April.

BTS won all three AMAs for which they were nominated, including Artist of the Year, Song of the Summer for "Swim", and Best Male K-Pop Artist. With their second Artist of the Year win, BTS is now tied with Justin Bieber and One Direction for the second-most wins in that category in AMA history, behind only Taylor Swift. Those victories pushed BTS's overall American Music Awards total to 14 career wins.

The night had real spectacle too. The seven-member outfit kicked off the Memorial Day awards show, held in Las Vegas, with a pre-taped performance of "Hooligan" from their concert the night before. Nearly 90 minutes after their performance aired at the top of the show, BTS took the stage at the MGM Grand Garden Arena to screaming fans in what marked the group's first appearance at an awards show in four years. BTS also presented the award for Best Female R&B Artist to SZA, who expressed her excitement over meeting the K-pop superstars.

And the good news keeps coming for BTS fans. In July, BTS will join Madonna and Shakira for the halftime show of the 2026 FIFA World Cup Final, marking the first-ever halftime show for the FIFA World Cup Final. They will co-headline alongside the pop stars on July 19 at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey. The AMAs wins feel less like a peak and more like a warm-up.
keyPoints: BTS won Artist of the Year, Song of the Summer for "SWIM", and Best Male K-Pop Artist at the 2026 AMAs
It was the group's first awards show appearance in four years following a hiatus for military service
"SWIM", from their album ARIRANG, reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100 in April 2026
The triple win brings BTS to 14 career AMA trophies, tying One Direction for the most Artist of the Year wins by a group
BTS will co-headline the first-ever FIFA World Cup Final halftime show on July 19 at MetLife Stadium with Madonna and Shakira

articles.title: Kith, adidas and Messi Drop a 20-Year Career Tribute Collection
articles.summary: Kith founder Ronnie Fieg and Lionel Messi spent a full year together designing a massive three-way collection with adidas Football, marking 20 years since Messi's World Cup debut and Kith's 15th anniversary. The collection spans on-pitch performance gear, luxury lifestyle apparel, and six footwear styles — all co-signed by Messi down to the last detail. It drops globally on Friday, May 29, and every jersey or shoe purchase comes with a limited-edition Messi trading card.
articles.content:
This is not a logo-slap collaboration. Every silhouette and style in Kith & Messi for adidas Football was designed collaboratively by Ronnie Fieg and Lionel Messi, with the two meeting multiple times over the course of a year to hand-curate the assortment and tailor each item to Messi's exact specifications. The result is one of the most personal drops either brand has ever put out.

The collection celebrates the 20th anniversary of Messi's debut on the world stage, as well as Kith's 15th anniversary, and covers both performance and lifestyle products. On the pitch side, the jerseys tell the story of a career. There are jerseys inspired by pivotal moments in Messi's career reimagined with Kith, adidas Football, and Messi motifs, including two 2006-inspired jerseys bearing Messi's original number 19, as well as two jerseys featuring his now-iconic number 10. Additional styles include all-over printed long-sleeve goalkeeper jerseys and gloves, tracksuits, nylon warm-up pullovers, socks, a ball bag, and two sizes of co-branded footballs.

The lifestyle half of the collection is where things get genuinely exciting for fashion fans. Off the pitch, the range leans into elevated tailoring and luxury sportswear through double-breasted suiting, wool tracksuits, washed denim sets, and premium accessories designed through Kith's lifestyle lens. Footwear plays a central role too, with a six-shoe lineup that includes the return of the adidas COPA Mundial 17 UltraBOOST from Kith and adidas Football's original 2017 partnership, alongside the futuristic adidas F50 Megaride, the archival-inspired F50 Walkable Cleat, two premium Handball Spezial colorways, and a deconstructed Superstar. The F50 models nod to the adidas cleats Messi wore during the 2006 FIFA World Cup, marking the beginning of his legendary run on football's biggest stage.

The campaign wraps everything up in a short film called The International Language of Messi, which pays tribute to the global impact and recognition of the icon's name. The film showcases commentators across multiple languages reacting to Messi's most iconic moments. Timing is everything here. The 2026 World Cup will in all likelihood be Messi's final tournament as a player, which makes this drop feel less like a merchandise moment and more like a genuine farewell celebration. Anyone who buys a jersey or a pair of shoes from the collection receives a limited-edition Messi trading card. Kith & Messi for adidas Football releases globally on Friday, May 29th at all Kith stores, as well as at 11AM on Kith.com and the Kith App in the New York, London, Paris, and Tokyo time zones.
keyPoints: Ronnie Fieg and Lionel Messi met repeatedly over a full year to co-design every piece in the collection
On-pitch jerseys feature both Messi's original number 19 from 2006 and his iconic number 10
Six footwear styles include the returning COPA Mundial 17 UltraBOOST, the F50 Megaride, the F50 Walkable Cleat, two Handball Spezial colorways, and a deconstructed Superstar
Every jersey or shoe purchase includes a limited-edition Messi trading card
The collection drops May 29 at Kith stores and Kith.com at 11AM across New York, London, Paris, and Tokyo time zones

articles.title: Sonny Rollins, Jazz's Saxophone Colossus, Dies at 95
articles.summary: Sonny Rollins, the tenor saxophonist widely regarded as America's greatest living jazz musician, died on May 25, 2026, at his home in Woodstock, New York. He was 95 and left behind a seven-decade career that reshaped the language of jazz improvisation. No cause of death was officially given, though his spokesperson noted he had been largely housebound in recent years due to various physical problems.
articles.content:
Sonny Rollins died on Sunday at his home in Woodstock, New York. He was 95. His publicist Terri Hinte announced his passing, and no specific cause of death was given. What is beyond dispute is what the world lost: the most commanding saxophone voice jazz has ever produced.

Rollins was born Theodore Walter Rollins on September 7, 1930, in Harlem, New York. His family had roots in the U.S. Virgin Islands, and that Caribbean heritage would colour his music for the rest of his life. He grew up in the Sugar Hill neighbourhood, where jazz was practically in the air, and by his teenage years he was mentored by the great pianist and composer Thelonious Monk, often rehearsing at Monk's apartment. He made his recording debut at just 18 in 1949 for Prestige Records.

His 1956 album Saxophone Colossus became one of the most celebrated records in jazz history. It was later selected for preservation by the National Recording Registry of the Library of Congress, a recognition reserved for recordings of cultural and historic importance. In a seven-decade career, Rollins recorded more than sixty albums as a leader, and the compositions he wrote along the way, including "St. Thomas", "Oleo", "Doxy" and "Airegin", became jazz standards that musicians still play all over the world today. He was often called "the greatest living improviser" and was one of the last surviving giants of the bebop era, a style of jazz that emerged in the 1940s and prized speed, complexity and bold individual expression.

Rollins earned the National Medal of Arts from President Barack Obama, the Kennedy Center Honors, a Grammy Award for lifetime achievement, and was designated a Jazz Master by the National Endowment for the Arts. He stopped performing more than a decade ago due to pulmonary fibrosis, a lung condition, but his music never stopped circulating. Even rock fans got a taste of his genius through the Rolling Stones' 1981 album Tattoo You, which features Rollins' wistful sax solo on the ballad "Waiting on a Friend." Jazz critic Gary Giddins once wrote that Rollins was "the most commanding musician alive." Today, that title belongs to history.
keyPoints: Sonny Rollins died on May 25, 2026 at age 95 at his home in Woodstock, New York
He was tutored by Thelonious Monk as a teenager and debuted on record at just 18
His 1956 album Saxophone Colossus was preserved by the Library of Congress National Recording Registry
He recorded more than sixty albums over a seven-decade career and wrote multiple jazz standards
Rollins received the National Medal of Arts, Grammy lifetime achievement award and Kennedy Center Honors

articles.title: Mandalorian and Grogu Is the Worst Star Wars Opening Under Disney
articles.summary: The Mandalorian and Grogu, Pedro Pascal's big-screen Star Wars debut, earned $82 million in its opening weekend and $100 million over the Memorial Day four-day frame, making it the lowest-opening Star Wars film since Disney acquired Lucasfilm in 2012. The result lands just below the previous record-holder, 2018's Solo: A Star Wars Story, which opened to $84 million and went on to become the first Star Wars film to lose money in theaters. The film carries a $165 million budget and will need between $500 and $600 million globally just to break even.
articles.content:
Star Wars is back on the big screen for the first time in seven years, and the opening numbers tell a complicated story. The Mandalorian and Grogu, directed by Jon Favreau and starring Pedro Pascal as bounty hunter Din Djarin alongside his beloved green sidekick Grogu, debuted to $82 million over its first three days and $100 million over the Memorial Day four-day holiday weekend. Those numbers beat analyst projections, but they still make it the worst Star Wars opening since Disney bought Lucasfilm back in 2012.

The previous low point was Solo: A Star Wars Story in 2018, which opened to $84 million and became the first Star Wars film ever to lose money at the box office, ultimately grossing $392 million worldwide against a bloated budget of roughly $300 million. The Mandalorian and Grogu is in a better position on cost, with a reported $165 million budget, but analysts say the film will need somewhere between $500 and $600 million globally just to break even. Globally it opened to $163 million, with $63 million coming from international markets across 51 territories.

The reviews have not helped. Critics delivered a rough reception, while audience sentiment online was described by social media analytics firm RelishMix as "mixed-negative leaning" and "more energized" than the positive side. Much of the chatter centered on a sense of "TV show stretched into a movie" fatigue. The Mandalorian and Grogu is a continuation of the three-season Disney+ streaming series The Mandalorian, and a significant chunk of potential moviegoers seem to be waiting to watch it at home, with 39% of PostTrak survey respondents saying they planned to watch it again on streaming rather than return to cinemas.

There are real reasons for cautious optimism though. The film earned an A-minus on CinemaScore exit polls, which is notably better than Solo's reception, and audience scores are holding up well. The budget is almost half what Solo cost. Star Wars is also one of the world's top toy franchises, generating roughly $1 billion in retail sales annually, and Grogu toys alone sold 13 million units during the first two years of the streaming series. The second weekend box office will be the real test of whether The Mandalorian and Grogu can leg it out to profitability or follow Solo into the record books for all the wrong reasons.
keyPoints: The Mandalorian and Grogu opened to $82 million domestically and $163 million globally over Memorial Day weekend
It is the worst-opening Star Wars film under Disney, narrowly below Solo: A Star Wars Story's $84 million in 2018
The film carries a $165 million budget and reportedly needs $500 to $600 million worldwide to break even
Critics gave the film poor reviews but audiences gave it an A-minus on CinemaScore
Significant online buzz pointed to "TV show stretched into a movie" fatigue and a preference for watching it on streaming

articles.title: TikTok's Text-to-Song Trend Is Turning Group Chats Into Hits
articles.summary: TikTok's #texttosong trend has everyday people using Suno's AI music app to transform their real text message conversations into full, genre-specific songs. The trend sent Suno's downloads quadrupling week over week in April 2026, briefly making it the number one music app on both the US and UK Apple App Stores. One Illinois mom, Justice Washam, racked up 9.8 million views and nearly 200,000 new followers after turning her 11-year-old daughter's texts into an Avril Lavigne-style banger.
articles.content:
Justice Washam is a 30-year-old travel agent from Illinois who had been posting on TikTok for almost a decade. Despite her 250,000 followers, as of early April the mother of three hadn't made much money off the platform, and her goal of hitting a million followers by the end of 2026 seemed far-fetched. Then her best friend spotted the #texttosong trend and knew exactly whose material to use.

Her best friend sent her a TikTok featuring screenshots of funny texts that had been turned into the lyrics of an AI-generated song, telling her: "Your daughter's messages are what this trend is made for!" Washam downloaded Suno's AI music generating app, pasted in her daughter's words, and requested a song in an early-2000s Avril Lavigne vein, saying "when I was her age, that kind of punk-pop music was popular." She made sure her daughter was OK with it, posted it, and went to bed. When she woke up it had already gotten a million views. Five weeks later it sat at 9.8 million views, and Washam had gained nearly 200,000 new followers.

The trend has become one of the hottest things on TikTok: friends, family members, acquaintances, and sometimes enemies turning their text histories into the lyrics to AI-generated songs. Suno saw downloads of its AI music app quadruple week over week in the US in April, briefly making it the most downloaded music app on both the US and UK Apple App Stores. Suno's product team even built a feature in about a week that partly automates converting text screenshots into songs, showing just how fast the company moved to capitalize on its own viral moment. Some of the songs produced this way are a little therapeutic, like one from a woman who made a Broadway-style song out of texts from a man asking her to refund him for dinner and an Uber because she didn't go home with him.

For Washam personally, the payoff has been real and financial. Because AI songs are longer than her usual posts, Washam says she's making more money than ever from ad sales on TikTok. Generally, content needs to be longer than one minute for creators to monetize it. In the last month or so, she has made $4,000. But the bigger question hanging over all of this is what it means for the music industry. The timing of the trend is serendipitous for Suno, which is in a legal battle with two of the three major record companies over how its model was trained, and who face distrust from swathes of the music industry. Research suggests that people using AI voice and music tools "are often more likely to engage more with fan-created versions of an entertainment than the entertainment itself," which is the kind of finding that keeps label executives up at night. Even Washam herself calls it her "5 minutes of fame," saying: "A few years from now, we're all gonna be like, do you guys remember when people were making songs with their text messages?" Whether or not she's right, the trend is already rewriting who gets to make music, and who gets to profit from it.
keyPoints: TikTok's #texttosong trend has everyday users turning real text conversations into AI-generated songs using Suno's app
Illinois creator Justice Washam's Avril Lavigne-style video of her daughter's texts hit 9.8 million views and earned her $4,000 in a month
Suno downloads quadrupled week over week in April 2026, making it the top music app on the US and UK Apple App Stores
Suno's product team built a text-screenshot-to-song automation feature in about a week to ride the trend
Suno is simultaneously fighting a legal battle with two of the three major record labels over how its AI model was trained

articles.title: Homer Gere's Wild Euphoria Debut: Explicit Scene with Sydney Sweeney
articles.summary: Homer Gere, 26-year-old son of Richard Gere, made headlines with his most daring scene yet in Euphoria Season 3 Episode 7, stripping off for a graphic sex scene alongside series regular Sydney Sweeney. The episode aired on HBO on May 24 and immediately went viral, sparking debate about Sweeney's character arc and earning Homer unexpected praise from viewers for his natural physique. It is Homer's first major TV role, and he certainly did not ease in gently.
articles.content:
If you were going to announce yourself to the world as an actor, doing it on Euphoria is one way to do it. Homer Gere, the 26-year-old son of Pretty Woman star Richard Gere, landed his first-ever major TV role in Euphoria Season 3, playing Dylan Reid, a rising television star who appears on the fictional show-within-a-show L.A. Nights. In Episode 7, which aired on HBO on May 24, he and Sydney Sweeney delivered the season's most talked-about scene yet.

Here is what actually happened in the episode. Sweeney's character Cassie Howard is desperate to raise money after deleting her OnlyFans account caused her to lose followers. Cassie and her friend Maddy, played by Alexa Demie, hatch a plan: go on a publicity date with Dylan Reid, sleep with him, and use the resulting social media moment to rebuild her following. The date ends back at Dylan's place, where the two launch into an intense hookup scene that reportedly left the room wrecked, including a broken bed and a painting knocked off the wall. Cassie then hacks Dylan's phone and posts a photo of them in bed with the caption "World's greatest f--- @itsjustmecassie," which goes viral.

Homer told Entertainment Tonight at the Euphoria Season 3 premiere back in April that this is his first real set experience, calling it "jumping into the deep end in a real cool way." He speaks with his father Richard every day by phone, even though Richard currently lives in Spain, and said his dad's advice is less about acting craft and more about how to carry yourself in the industry. Viewers online were quick to notice Homer's more natural physique compared to the typical Hollywood leading man, and many praised him warmly for it.

The scene has also reignited a bigger conversation about what Euphoria is doing with Cassie this season. Creator Sam Levinson has leaned hard into an explicit storyline for the character, and some fans and PR experts have pushed back, arguing it risks narrowing Sydney Sweeney's public image. Crisis management expert Dave Quast noted that Sweeney's ability to generate headlines has real commercial value, but warned that the challenge is keeping the focus on her acting rather than the sexualised aspects of the role alone. Euphoria Season 3 concludes with its finale on May 31.

As for Homer Gere, Euphoria is just the beginning. He is also set to appear in the upcoming FX TV adaptation of The Shards later this summer, where he was reportedly spotted filming alongside Kaia Gerber, Cindy Crawford's daughter. Not a bad start for a first-time TV actor.
keyPoints: Homer Gere, son of Richard Gere, plays Dylan Reid in Euphoria Season 3 and had his most explicit scene yet in Episode 7
Sydney Sweeney's character Cassie Howard uses the hookup to try to rebuild her OnlyFans following after losing fans
Homer Gere received widespread praise from viewers for his natural physique, bucking Hollywood body standards
PR experts have warned the increasingly explicit Cassie storyline could narrow Sydney Sweeney's career options
Homer is set to appear next in the FX adaptation of The Shards later in 2026

articles.title: Cox Media Fined for Selling Fake Phone-Spying Ads That Never Worked
articles.summary: The FTC has fined Cox Media Group $880,000 after the company sold a service called Active Listening that claimed to eavesdrop on consumers through their phones and smart devices to serve targeted ads — a capability the FTC confirmed never actually existed. Cox Media Group, along with marketing firms MindSift LLC and 1010 Digital Works LLC, must pay a combined $930,000 to settle the charges. The real product was nothing more than a repackaged consumer email list dressed up with AI branding.
articles.content:
You know that feeling when you mention shoes to a friend and suddenly see shoe ads everywhere on your phone? Most of us have joked that our phones are listening. Turns out, Cox Media Group decided to turn that paranoia into a product — and then got caught.

Cox Media Group marketed an 'Active Listening' service that claimed to use artificial intelligence to detect conversations from smart devices. The Federal Trade Commission has confirmed that the service never collected a single piece of voice data. Not one word. Not one whisper. The whole thing was fiction.

The marketing product called Active Listening was pitched as a novel algorithm that could take snippets from user conversations, supposedly overheard by their smart home devices, and use them to generate targeted ads to other users in specific geographic regions. The FTC complaint says Cox Media Group employees asked prospective clients 'Where do you want us to listen?' and cited products and platforms including Alexa, Google, Samsung and OpenTable as examples of data sources feeding the system. They even had a slogan for it. Cox Media Group marketing materials read 'Don't Just Know What They're Searching For — Know What They're Talking About,' while one promotional message acknowledged the privacy implications head-on, asking: 'Creepy? Sure. Great for marketing? Definitely.'

So what was the service actually doing? The service was, in fact, a resale operation built on purchased consumer email lists. Advertisers were told they could reach people within specific local radiuses, but customer lists often contained consumers from across the country rather than the promised nearby markets. Small businesses paid for cutting-edge AI surveillance and got a generic mailing list.

The FTC announced on 21 May 2026 that Cox Media Group, along with MindSift LLC and 1010 Digital Works LLC, will pay a combined $930,000 to settle charges of deceptive marketing. Cox Media Group will pay $880,000, while MindSift and 1010 Digital Works will each pay $25,000. All three companies are also barred from misrepresenting the features of their marketing services, collecting voice data, and geographic targeting capabilities. In a statement, Cox Media Group said it is 'pleased to have this matter resolved' and that its local marketing team 'relied on marketing materials provided to us by a third-party vendor.' The company said it withdrew the materials and stopped using the product.

The case adds to the FTC's growing enforcement record against deceptive AI claims. The agency launched Operation AI Comply in September 2024 to target companies that use AI hype to mislead consumers and businesses. The Cox Media Group settlement shows that regulators will not just pursue companies that misuse AI, but also those that falsely claim to use it. The era of slapping 'AI-powered' on anything and charging a premium is getting a lot shorter.
keyPoints: Cox Media Group's Active Listening service claimed to eavesdrop via phones and smart devices to target ads, but the FTC confirmed it never collected any voice data
The actual product was a repackaged consumer email list, not an AI surveillance tool
Cox Media Group must pay $880,000, with marketing partners MindSift LLC and 1010 Digital Works LLC paying $25,000 each
All three companies are now banned from making false claims about voice data collection and geographic targeting
The FTC's Operation AI Comply, launched in September 2024, is actively targeting companies that fake AI capabilities

articles.title: Estée Lauder and Puig's $40B Merger Died With a Skull Emoji
articles.summary: Estée Lauder and Spanish beauty giant Puig were days away from completing a $40 billion merger that would have created the world's largest premium beauty company, before the whole deal collapsed on 21 May 2026. Leaks, family feuds, and a last-minute contract demand from Charlotte Tilbury killed it. The whole thing ended when advisers texted each other a skull emoji.
articles.content:
This was supposed to be a beauty industry blockbuster. Estée Lauder, the American giant behind Clinique, MAC Cosmetics, and Tom Ford Beauty, was in deep negotiations with Puig, the Spanish fragrance and fashion conglomerate that owns Carolina Herrera and Charlotte Tilbury. Together they would have formed a $40 billion luxury beauty empire. Months of talks took place across Paris, New York, and Barcelona. At several points the two sides had reached agreements in principle on major issues.

Then everything fell apart. On the night of Thursday 21 May 2026, Puig chairman Marc Puig and Estée Lauder chairman William Lauder got on a phone call described by insiders as a conversation about "a deteriorating situation." Shortly after that call ended, advisers on both sides began messaging each other. One of those messages contained a skull emoji. The deal was dead.

So what went wrong? Three things, according to Reuters sources with direct knowledge of the process. First, details of the talks leaked, creating pressure and public scrutiny neither side wanted at a delicate stage. Second, the two powerful controlling families — the Lauder family on one side, the Puig family on the other — couldn't resolve disagreements over how the combined company would be structured and who would control key assets. Third, Charlotte Tilbury herself reportedly wanted to renegotiate her contract. Tilbury sold her eponymous brand to Puig in 2020 but holds a minority stake until 2031, and she reportedly pushed for new terms before agreeing to be part of the merged entity.

The market reaction said everything. When the merger talks first went public, Puig shares rose while Estée Lauder stock fell, suggesting investors were more excited for Puig than Estée Lauder. But the moment the deal collapsed, the script flipped: Estée Lauder shares jumped around 10% while Puig dropped approximately 13%. Investors were essentially saying they are happier with Estée Lauder focusing on its own turnaround than gambling on a mega-merger. For Puig, losing the deal stings more because Charlotte Tilbury is one of its most profitable and high-profile assets.

For beauty fans, the collapse means the dream of one roof housing Tom Ford, Clinique, MAC, Carolina Herrera, and Charlotte Tilbury is not happening anytime soon. Charlotte Tilbury in particular has become a phenomenon in its own right, beloved by TikTok influencers and millennials alike. Whether she pursues a different path for her brand from here is now a very interesting question.
keyPoints: Estée Lauder and Puig abandoned a merger that would have created a $40 billion beauty group
The deal collapsed on 21 May 2026 after leaks, family disagreements, and Charlotte Tilbury's contract demands
The end was signalled by a skull emoji sent between advisers after a call between Marc Puig and William Lauder
After the collapse, Estée Lauder shares rose around 10% while Puig shares fell around 13%
The merged company would have united Tom Ford, Clinique, MAC, Carolina Herrera, and Charlotte Tilbury under one roof

articles.title: Jack Osbourne: AI Ozzy Is "Not Gonna Be F***ing Lame"
articles.summary: Jack Osbourne has fired back at critics of the family's plan to bring Ozzy Osbourne back as an AI-powered digital avatar, insisting the project is far more sophisticated than simply plugging his dad's image into ChatGPT. The Osbourne family partnered with digital human tech company Hyperreal to build what Jack calls "the digital DNA of Ozzy Osbourne" after Ozzy's death in July 2025 at age 76. Life-sized interactive versions of the avatar are set to begin appearing on Proto Luma holoportation screens across the US and UK in late summer 2026.
articles.content:
Jack Osbourne is not here for the criticism. After announcing plans to bring his late father Ozzy Osbourne back as an AI-powered digital avatar, Jack jumped on a YouTube livestream to defend the project and make one thing very clear: this is not some cheap cash-grab.

"Here's the thing, it's gonna be so tasteful what we're doing. It's not gonna be f***ing lame," he said. "It's really complex what we're doing. This isn't just like hooking up an image of my dad to ChatGPT. This is some high-level technology that we're gonna be working with, and it's gonna feel very real."

Ozzy, the iconic Black Sabbath frontman, passed away in July 2025 at the age of 76. The AI avatar project was revealed at the Licensing Expo in Las Vegas on May 20, alongside Sharon Osbourne. The Osbourne family has partnered with digital human tech company Hyperreal to create an Ozzy avatar with the ability to "have conversations with fans and move, speak, and respond as Ozzy would," according to Hyperreal.

The AI-powered version of Ozzy will appear through Proto Hologram and their Proto Luma "holoportation" devices, which are designed to project life-sized digital avatars capable of interactive conversations. The Ozzy avatar will begin appearing in life-sized, interactive touchscreens in the US and UK beginning in late summer 2026. Sharon Osbourne described the experience simply: "You can ask Ozzy anything, and he will answer you in his own voice. We're going to take it all around the world."

Some fans called the idea upsetting, arguing that it turns Ozzy's memory into a business product too soon after his passing. But Jack pushed back on that framing too. "It's really cool, and it's something that I think my dad would be into," Jack said. "We actually talked about it before he passed... I know he would be into this."

Jack described the project as capturing "the digital DNA of Ozzy Osbourne, voice, image [and] movement," adding that "the things that you can do with that are just endless." Hyperreal also went through this process with the late Stan Lee, whose hologram debuted at LA Comic Con in 2025. Sharon summed up the family's vision by pointing to the ultimate rock and roll benchmark: "Elvis died 50 years ago, and everybody knows Elvis. I just want that for Ozzy."
keyPoints: Jack Osbourne defended the AI Ozzy Osbourne avatar project in a YouTube livestream
The Osbourne family partnered with Hyperreal to build interactive AI avatars of Ozzy
The avatars will appear on Proto Luma holoportation screens in the US and UK from late summer 2026
Ozzy passed away in July 2025 at age 76
Jack says he and Ozzy discussed the idea before Ozzy died and that Ozzy would have approved

articles.title: Alamo Drafthouse Adds In-Movie Phone Ordering, Fans Are Furious
articles.summary: Alamo Drafthouse, the cinema chain famous for its strict no-phones-ever policy, has rolled out a mandatory mobile ordering system that requires customers to use their phones during films to order food and drinks. Loyal fans are calling it a betrayal of everything the chain stood for, with a petition to bring back pen-and-paper ordering gathering over 1,300 signatures. One superfan in Denver even launched a boycott movement called Operation Save the Sanctuary.
articles.content:
Alamo Drafthouse built its entire identity on one simple promise: sit down, shut up, and put your phone away. For decades, the Austin-born cinema chain ran pre-show videos featuring celebrities telling audiences to turn their phones off, with Dolly Parton being one of the most recent stars to deliver that message. Staff would actually remove people from screenings for breaking the rule. It was the whole point.

So when Alamo Drafthouse CEO Michael Kustermann announced a new policy replacing the classic pen-and-paper food ordering system with a QR-code-based mobile app, fans lost it. The old system was beautifully simple: scribble your order on a slip of paper, a server picks it up, and your phone never comes out. Now, customers are required to use the Alamo Drafthouse app to order food and drinks both before and during films.

The backlash was immediate and loud. Elijah Wood, The Lord of the Rings star and an Austin local, called the move "a profound and upsetting mistake" and said it was "completely antithetical to the ethos of the Alamo." A petition asking Alamo Drafthouse to reinstate analog ordering gathered more than 1,300 signatures. Posts about the change on Austin's subreddit hit over 1,000 upvotes, with comments describing the shift as "devastating" and "genuinely terrible." On Reddit, one fan put it plainly: "Encouraging people to pull out their phones during movies is the exact opposite" of everything Alamo stands for.

Alamo Drafthouse says the app uses "dark-screen technology" designed to stay dim and avoid distracting other moviegoers, and that talking, texting, and social media scrolling are still banned. The company also argues the new system will reduce disruptions by eliminating late-film check-ins by servers. But fans are unconvinced. In Denver, a superfan named Twich Collins who has seen 135 films at Alamo Drafthouse in a single year launched Operation Save the Sanctuary, a movement where loyal patrons still attend screenings but refuse to order food or drink, hitting Alamo Drafthouse where it hurts most: concession revenue. Movie theaters make the bulk of their money on food and drink, not ticket sales.

The story goes a little deeper than just phones. Since Sony Pictures acquired Alamo Drafthouse in 2024, workers at several locations have gone on strike over job cuts. The Denver Sloan's Lake location saw a union strike specifically over the mobile ordering rollout, with employees reporting their hours being cut and workloads going up. Alamo Drafthouse insists no jobs will be lost because of mobile ordering. But given the recent history, a lot of fans and workers are not taking that promise at face value.
keyPoints: Alamo Drafthouse replaced its iconic pen-and-paper food ordering with a mandatory QR code phone app
Elijah Wood and the Austin Film Critics Association publicly condemned the change
A petition to reinstate analog ordering surpassed 1,300 signatures
Denver superfan Twich Collins launched Operation Save the Sanctuary, a concession boycott
Workers at the Sloan's Lake Denver location went on strike over the rollout and fears of job cuts

articles.title: Spotify Reserved Uses Your Stream History as a Concert Ticket
articles.summary: Spotify has launched Reserved, a new feature for eligible Spotify Premium subscribers in the US that uses your listening data, including streams and shares, to identify an artist's most dedicated fans and hold up to two concert tickets for them before general sale. The feature is backed by a multiyear deal with Live Nation and launches this summer with select tours. Spotify says there are no added fees from Spotify itself for the service.
articles.content:
Buying concert tickets in 2026 is still a nightmare. You set an alarm, you spam refresh, and you watch seats disappear in seconds while bots and scalpers scoop everything up. Spotify thinks your listening history should be the thing that saves you a seat.

Spotify has introduced Reserved, a new experience for eligible Spotify Premium subscribers ages 18 and up, starting in the US. With Reserved, Spotify identifies an artist's most dedicated fans and holds two tour tickets for them, with no racing against thousands of other fans and no hunting for codes — just a reserved window to purchase before tickets go on sale to the general public.

The mechanics are straightforward. Eligibility is based on signals such as streams, shares, and wider in-app activity, alongside safeguards to reduce bot activity and prioritize real listeners. Once selected, fans are notified via email and in-app alerts and can purchase up to two tickets through a partner platform during their designated window. That dedicated window lasts about a day. Reserved offers are based on the tour's locations, so if a tour is not coming to your area, you may not receive an offer.

Spotify has partnered with Live Nation on the program as part of a multiyear agreement. Live Nation is the world's largest concert promoter and parent company of Ticketmaster, so getting them on board matters enormously for how widely Reserved can actually reach fans. Spotify already partners with more than 40 ticketing platforms and has driven more than $1.5 billion in ticket sales through its concert-related features.

Starting this summer, newly announced tours for select artists will be able to dedicate tickets to true fans through Reserved, with plans to expand to more tours of all sizes, including many of the most sought-after shows on the road. Spotify says there are no added fees from Spotify itself, and the aim is that more of an artist's truest fans will end up in the crowd where it matters most.

The real question is scale. Beyond scalpers, a major reason why it is so difficult to buy tickets is that ticket pools have become increasingly fragmented into smaller and smaller pre-sale pots. Reserved is technically another presale pool, just one powered by genuine fan data rather than credit card partnerships or radio station codes. Whether that changes the experience in a meaningful way will depend entirely on how many tickets artists actually choose to put into the program. But as a concept, turning your streaming history into a ticket queue is a genuinely new idea and one that could reshape what it means to be a loyal fan in the streaming era.
keyPoints: Spotify Reserved holds up to two concert tickets for an artist's most dedicated Spotify Premium subscribers before general sale
Eligibility is determined by streams, shares, and in-app activity, with bot-detection safeguards built in
Fans get about a day to purchase their reserved tickets through a third-party ticketing partner
Spotify has signed a multiyear deal with Live Nation to power the feature
Reserved launches in the US this summer with select tours, with more markets and artists to follow

articles.title: Doctor Who's Christmas Special Is Hanging in the Balance Over a Casting Crisis
articles.summary: The BBC's planned 2026 Doctor Who Christmas special is reportedly in serious jeopardy because showrunner Russell T Davies and BBC bosses cannot find anyone willing to play the Sixteenth Doctor. The role, once one of the most coveted jobs in British television, is now being described by insiders as a poisoned chalice following the troubled Ncuti Gatwa era. With less than seven months until Christmas Day, no lead actor has been announced and there are no signs that production has even started.
articles.content:
Doctor Who has been a Christmas institution in the UK since the show was revived in 2005. Every December 25th, millions of families sat down to watch the Doctor's latest adventure. So the idea that the 2026 Christmas special might not happen at all is a genuinely big deal for British television.

According to a report from The Sun, the special is now "hanging in the balance" because the BBC and Russell T Davies have not been able to lock down a new actor to play the Sixteenth Doctor. The problem, according to an insider source, is that the role no longer feels like the career win it once was. The source described it as coming with "all the baggage of the most recent series," which is a politely brutal way of saying the Ncuti Gatwa era did not go as planned.

To understand why, a bit of backstory helps. Ncuti Gatwa took over as the Fifteenth Doctor in a high-profile era backed by Disney Plus, which came on board as an international distribution partner to give Doctor Who a bigger global footprint and a larger budget. The partnership did not deliver the hoped-for breakthrough in the US market, and Disney Plus has since stepped away from future involvement with the show. To make things stranger, Gatwa's run ended with a regeneration into Billie Piper, something that was later confirmed not to be a canonical new Doctor but a creative favour between Piper and Davies. That left the identity of the actual next Doctor completely unresolved.

The BBC officially announced late in 2025 that Davies had agreed to write a Christmas special for 2026, and the corporation said it remained fully committed to the show. But as of today, with fewer than seven months to go, there has been no casting announcement and no sign that cameras have rolled. An insider also warned that even if something is pulled together in time, there are fears it could end up feeling like, in their words, "a poor cousin to previous Christmas specials."

There is also a complicating factor on the creative side. Davies has confirmed he will be leaving Doctor Who after this special, which means he is unlikely to be the one choosing the next long-term Doctor alongside a new showrunner. One alternative being floated is that the special could shift from Christmas 2026 to Easter 2027, which would ease the production timeline pressure. For now though, one of Britain's most beloved TV traditions is genuinely up in the air, and that is a story worth watching closely.
keyPoints: The 2026 Doctor Who Christmas special is reportedly at risk due to a casting crisis over the Sixteenth Doctor
Industry insiders describe the Doctor Who lead role as a poisoned chalice following the Ncuti Gatwa era
Disney Plus backed the recent seasons but has now stepped away from the show
Russell T Davies confirmed he is leaving Doctor Who after this special
The special could potentially shift from Christmas 2026 to Easter 2027 if casting is not resolved in time

articles.title: Ozempic Is Breaking Fashion's Broken Sizing System
articles.summary: GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic are doing what decades of body-positivity campaigns could not: forcing fashion brands to confront how badly their sizing and inventory systems actually work. As millions of people change shape rapidly and unpredictably, retailers are being caught flat-footed with the wrong stock at the wrong time. The disruption is real, but so is the opportunity for brands willing to move fast.
articles.content:
Fashion has always had a sizing problem. Vanity sizing, inconsistent measurements across brands, and a wholesale model built around rigid size runs have been the industry's dirty secrets for years. But Ozempic and other GLP-1 drugs are making those secrets impossible to ignore.

The numbers are staggering. Millions of people around the world are now on GLP-1 medications, and many of them are losing weight faster than a seasonal wardrobe cycle can keep up with. That means a shopper who bought a size 12 dress in autumn might need a size 8 by spring. Brands built around slow inventory planning simply were not designed for that kind of rapid body change.

The disruption hits in two painful places. First, fit. Most garments are designed around a sample size and scaled up or down using formulas that already did not work perfectly. When a customer's proportions shift in unexpected ways, those scaling assumptions break down even faster. A person losing weight on a GLP-1 drug does not shrink uniformly. Shoulders, waists, and hips all change at different rates, which means standard alterations often are not enough.

Second, inventory. Retailers order stock months in advance based on historical sales data. That data is now unreliable. A store that used to sell twice as many size 14s as size 10s might find those ratios flipping mid-season with no warning. The brands that are already experimenting with on-demand manufacturing, more flexible size ranges, and smarter real-time data tools are the ones best positioned to turn this disruption into a genuine competitive edge. The ones that wait risk being left with warehouses full of the wrong sizes and customers who have simply moved on.
keyPoints: GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic are causing rapid body changes that fashion's slow inventory cycles cannot keep up with
Standard size scaling formulas break down when customers lose weight unevenly across different body areas
Retailers face unsold stock problems because historical size sales data is becoming unreliable
Brands experimenting with on-demand manufacturing and real-time data tools have the most to gain
The crisis is exposing sizing flaws that have existed in fashion for decades

articles.title: Ferrari Luce Gets Roasted Online: Nissan Leaf Comparisons Are Flying
articles.summary: Ferrari's first EV, the Ferrari Luce, had barely been unveiled before the internet started dunking on its looks, with commenters comparing the €550,000 car to a Nissan Leaf and a Kia. The Luce's unusual light blue colour and its tall, glasshouse silhouette are at the centre of the jokes. It is a fascinating culture moment: a half-million-euro car getting the same treatment as a budget hatchback.
articles.content:
The Ferrari Luce is officially out in the world, and the internet has officially taken its shot. Within hours of Ferrari pulling back the curtain in Rome, social media lit up with one very specific set of comparisons. Ferrari says the Luce was "designed with Sir Jony Ive and Marc Newson at the creative collective LoveFrom," with a design language that unites the exterior, interior, and interface with "clarity and refined simplicity." The internet's verdict? It looks like something you would see in a supermarket car park.

The roasting has been fast and creative. People online are comparing the Ferrari Luce, which comes in a light blue, to a Nissan Leaf and a Kia. Both of those cars cost a fraction of the Ferrari Luce's €550,000 starting price, which makes the comparison sting even more. The Luce's sweeping glasshouse roof and its five-door, five-seat, sedan-like proportions are a genuine departure from everything Ferrari has ever made, and that departure is clearly giving people whiplash.

To be fair, this was always going to happen. The Luce arrives as Ferrari's first fully electric car, but the biggest shock is visual. Shown in Rome after a staged reveal, the car stretches Ferrari into unfamiliar territory: five seats, a sedan-like profile, rear-hinged doors, and a form that asks the eye to recalibrate before the badge fully lands. Ferrari has never made a car like this. The brand built its entire legend on low-slung, two-seat machines that sounded like the apocalypse. The Luce is quiet, roomy, and shaped like a rolling greenhouse.

The comparison game is actually a pretty useful cultural thermometer. When a car that costs as much as a house gets compared to a Nissan Leaf, it tells you something about how hard it is to shift brand expectations. As big a departure as the Luce's interior is from Ferrari's current suite of sports cars, the exterior is an even bigger step, one that not everyone is going to love. Ferrari is betting that in a few years this will look visionary. Right now it is looking like the set-up to a very expensive punchline.
keyPoints: The Ferrari Luce's light blue exterior and glasshouse roofline are drawing Nissan Leaf and Kia comparisons online
The €550,000 starting price makes the budget-car comparisons hit especially hard
The Luce is Ferrari's first five-seat, four-door car, a radical break from the brand's two-seat sports car heritage
The backlash reflects how difficult it is to shift a luxury brand's visual identity even with a world-class designer involved
Jony Ive and Marc Newson designed the entire car, exterior included, through their studio LoveFrom

articles.title: Fergie Reunites With will.i.am, apl.de.ap and Taboo at 2026 AMAs
articles.summary: Fergie had an emotional on-stage reunion with her former Black Eyed Peas bandmates will.i.am, apl.de.ap, and Taboo at the 2026 American Music Awards. The reunion is a big deal because Fergie left the group back in 2018 to focus on her solo career, making this their first major public appearance together in years. Fans who grew up on 'Big Girls Don't Cry' and 'Boom Boom Pow' had plenty of reasons to get emotional right along with them.
articles.content:
Fergie and her Black Eyed Peas brothers are back together. The 'Big Girls Don't Cry' singer joined will.i.am, apl.de.ap, and Taboo on stage at the 2026 American Music Awards, and by all accounts it was a genuinely moving moment. The American Music Awards, known as the AMAs, are one of the biggest fan-voted music award shows in the United States, so having this reunion play out in front of that crowd made it feel even more special.

Fergie had stepped away from the Black Eyed Peas back in 2018, citing a desire to focus on her solo career. The group carried on as a trio, releasing music and touring without her. That eight-year gap made the 2026 AMAs moment hit differently, not just for fans but clearly for the artists themselves.

The word 'brothers' says a lot. Fergie used it herself to describe will.i.am, apl.de.ap, and Taboo, which tells you this was not just a professional reunion. There is real history and real affection between these four people. They built one of the biggest-selling pop acts of the 2000s together, racking up hits like 'I Gotta Feeling' and 'Where Is the Love?' before Fergie's departure.

Whether this reunion is a one-off moment or a sign of something bigger coming is still unclear. But given the emotional weight everyone seemed to bring to it, the idea of new Black Eyed Peas music with Fergie back in the fold does not feel so far-fetched right now.
keyPoints: Fergie reunited with will.i.am, apl.de.ap, and Taboo at the 2026 AMAs
Fergie left the Black Eyed Peas in 2018 to pursue her solo career
The reunion was described as emotional by those present
Fergie referred to her former bandmates as her brothers
It is unclear whether the reunion signals new music or was a one-time event

articles.title: UK Films Star More Chrises Than Women Over 60, Study Finds
articles.summary: A new analysis by the Centre for Ageing Better found that among the 100 highest-grossing UK films of the past three years, more featured a lead actor named Chris than a woman over 60. Only five films in that entire period starred a woman over 60 as the lead, while six starred a Chris — and films were four times more likely to feature a talking animal in the lead role than an older woman. Emma Thompson, aged 67, is calling for change.
articles.content:
Here is a number that should make Hollywood squirm. Out of the 100 biggest films at the UK box office across 2023, 2024 and 2025, just five starred a woman over the age of 60 in the lead role. Five. That is the finding from the Centre for Ageing Better's Age Without Limits campaign, which set out to measure how badly older women are being shut out of mainstream cinema.

The comparison that really stings? Six of those same 100 films starred a lead actor named Chris. Half of those Chrises were Chris Pratt, who led The Super Mario Bros. Movie, Guardians of the Galaxy Volume 3, and The Garfield Movie. And if you think that is bad, the analysis also found that films were four times more likely to feature a talking animal in a lead role than a woman over 60. A fictional creature gets more screen time than a real, living woman past 60.

Emma Thompson, who is 67 and has an Oscar to her name, did not hold back. She called older women "compelling, relatable" and said they are "overdue for centre stage." Her message to studios was simple: older women do not need permission to exist on screen, because they already exist in the world. Cinema just needs to catch up. Dr Carole Easton OBE, Chief Executive at the Centre for Ageing Better, called the situation "absolutely ludicrous" and pointed out that up to one in five UK cinema attendees are aged 55 and above, a group that spends hundreds of millions of pounds on cinema every year.

The disconnect between the audience and the screen is striking. The women who did make the cut in those five films include names like Demi Moore, Diane Keaton, and Jamie Lee Curtis. Meanwhile, 76-year-old Meryl Streep's Devil Wears Prada sequel has already crossed $600 million at the global box office in 2026, proving that audiences absolutely will show up for older women when studios give them the chance. The data is there. The money is there. The only thing missing is the will.
keyPoints: Only 5 of the 100 top UK films from 2023 to 2025 starred a woman over 60 as the lead
6 films in that same group starred a lead actor named Chris, half of them Chris Pratt
Films were four times more likely to feature a talking animal lead than a woman over 60
Emma Thompson, 67, is publicly calling on studios to put more older women at the centre of stories
Up to one in five UK cinema-goers are aged 55 or older, a group spending hundreds of millions on tickets annually

articles.title: Calbee Goes Grayscale as Hormuz Crisis Hits Chip Bag Color
articles.summary: Japanese snack giant Calbee is stripping the color from 14 of its most beloved products, switching to monochrome packaging because of supply disruptions caused by the Strait of Hormuz blockade. The shortage affects petroleum-based printing inks, specifically naphtha, a byproduct of oil that has become hard to source since the waterway closed. New grayscale bags started hitting shelves the week of May 25, 2026.
articles.content:
Calbee is one of Japan's most recognized snack brands. Its bright orange-and-yellow chip bags are practically a cultural landmark in Tokyo convenience stores. But as of this week, those iconic colors are going dark.

On Tuesday, Tokyo-based snack company Calbee announced a creative response to supply chain disruptions caused by the blockade in the Strait of Hormuz, switching its brightly colored packaging to monochrome. Calbee will temporarily convert its colorful packaging to grayscale for 14 product variants, including its potato chips, Kappa Ebisen shrimp crackers, and Frugra granola snacks. Buyers can expect to see the temporary packaging starting the week of May 25.

So why does a geopolitical crisis halfway around the world change the color of your chip bag? The change is driven by shortages of petroleum-based materials used in printing inks, particularly naphtha, a key industrial input linked to oil supply chains strained by the ongoing conflict and disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz. Companies around the world have been scrambling to find solutions to supply disruptions since the US and Israel attacked Iran in late February, prompting the prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a major shipping route and source of Middle Eastern oil to the rest of the world.

Calbee emphasized that the contents of its snacks will remain unchanged, with only the packaging affected as it works to stabilize production and manage material shortages. The snacks taste exactly the same. Calbee is framing the move as a practical workaround, not a downgrade. Still, it is a striking visual sign of just how far the ripple effects of the Hormuz crisis have spread.

These disruptions have already played out in different sectors, with fertilizer shortages hurting farmers across Asia and Indian rice producers unable to export their grain. Calbee's grayscale bags are the kind of everyday, tangible reminder that global conflicts touch ordinary life in unexpected ways. A bag of chips at a Tokyo 7-Eleven has now become a front-row seat to one of the biggest geopolitical crises of 2026.
keyPoints: Calbee is temporarily switching 14 snack product lines to grayscale packaging due to supply shortages
The shortage traces back to naphtha, a petroleum byproduct used in printing inks, cut off by the Strait of Hormuz blockade
The Strait of Hormuz has been closed since the US and Israel attacked Iran in late February 2026
The snack contents are completely unchanged, only the bag designs are affected
Grayscale Calbee bags began appearing on shelves the week of May 25, 2026

articles.title: Jim Henson's Creature Shop Finally Opens Its Doors in Queens
articles.summary: Jim Henson's Creature Shop, the legendary puppet workshop behind the Muppets, Sesame Street, and Fraggle Rock, is now welcoming the public for behind-the-scenes tours in Long Island City, Queens for the first time in its history. The 80-minute Saturday tours cost $150 per person and include a live puppetry demonstration, a meet-and-greet with a puppet builder, and photo opportunities with iconic characters. For decades this place was a total secret — now you can walk right in.
articles.content:
Jim Henson's Creature Shop has spent decades quietly building some of the most beloved characters on earth. Kermit the Frog, Oscar the Grouch, the Fraggle Rock gang, the creatures from The Dark Crystal — they all came to life inside a warehouse in Queens, New York, and almost nobody knew it. That changes now.

This year, Jim Henson's Creature Shop opened its Queens workshop to public tours for the first time. The 80-minute Saturday tours cost $150 per person and include a live puppetry demonstration from an actual Henson puppeteer, a meet-and-greet with one of the shop's puppet builders, and photo opportunities with characters from the shows and films that made the studio famous.

Jason Weber, the shop's creative supervisor, said the tours are an opportunity to celebrate the unsung craftspeople who bring these characters to life. And those craftspeople are passionate about their work. Senior puppet builder Sierra Schoening said the job had been her "pie-in-the-sky" dream, crediting Henson's 1986 fantasy film Labyrinth with first sparking her fascination. "I know all the secret sauce, and I'm making the secret sauce now," she said.

Jim Henson originally founded the workshop in Manhattan in the 1960s, and it has moved several times since. It has been at its current location at 37-18 Northern Boulevard in Long Island City since 2009. The shop is also responsible for recent work including the Emmy-winning Fraggle Rock: Back to the Rock on Apple TV, the Five Nights at Freddy's horror franchise, and an upcoming all-puppet episode of Star Trek: Strange New Worlds. Tickets for Saturday tours through September 26, 2026 are on sale now at JimHensonExperiences.com.
keyPoints: Jim Henson's Creature Shop in Queens is open for public tours for the first time ever
Tours run every Saturday, last 80 minutes, and cost $150 per person
Visitors get a live puppetry demo, a puppet builder meet-and-greet, and character photo ops
The Queens workshop has been the home of the shop since 2009
Recent credits include the Emmy-winning Fraggle Rock: Back to the Rock and Five Nights at Freddy's

articles.title: Sweden Compares France's Zyn Ban to Banning Baguettes
articles.summary: France has officially banned Zyn and all other nicotine pouches, making simple possession a criminal offense that can land you up to five years in prison and a fine of around $436,600. Sweden's Trade Minister Benjamin Dousa fired back, saying banning Zyn is like Sweden banning French baguettes or French wine. It is the harshest nicotine pouch crackdown anywhere in the world, and cigarettes are still perfectly legal in France.
articles.content:
France dropped one of the most aggressive nicotine bans on the planet, and it kicked in on April 1, 2026. The target? Zyn and every other nicotine pouch brand you can think of. Not just off shelves. Off the streets entirely.

The French health ministry has classified nicotine in oral products as a "toxic substance," and the penalties are not subtle. The new regulation imposes criminal penalties on the use, possession, purchase, and sale of nicotine pouches. Violators can face up to five years in prison and fines of up to €400,000 (approximately $436,600). To put that in perspective, that is roughly the same punishment you might expect for trafficking serious controlled substances in many countries.

European nations including Austria, Belgium, Germany, Luxembourg and the Netherlands have all moved to ban or tightly restrict the sale of nicotine pouches, but France is so far the only country to criminalize their use or possession. And the irony is hard to miss. The ban does not apply to cigarettes or vape products. So you can smoke a pack of cigarettes outside a Parisian café without a second glance, but tuck a Zyn under your lip and you could theoretically end up in handcuffs.

Sweden was not going to stay quiet about this. Zyn is a Swedish product, made by Swedish Match, and nicotine pouches trace their roots directly to Scandinavian snus culture. Swedish Trade Minister Benjamin Dousa hit back at France's crackdown, saying it is like banning French baguettes or French wine in Sweden. That comparison landed hard online and summed up exactly why this story blew up.

The French government's reasoning centers on youth protection. Nicotine pouches have been growing in popularity across Europe, and France has been watching the trend with concern. The colorful packaging, fruity and minty flavors, and discreet format make these products attractive to younger demographics. That is a fair point. But critics argue that criminalizing adult possession while leaving cigarettes untouched is logic that falls apart pretty fast. Many adults use nicotine pouches as an alternative to smoking, which makes the selective approach all the harder to defend.
keyPoints: France banned Zyn and all nicotine pouches from April 1, 2026, criminalizing possession and use
Penalties reach up to five years in prison and a $436,600 fine — the harshest in the world
Cigarettes and vapes are still fully legal in France under the same law
Swedish Trade Minister Benjamin Dousa compared the ban to banning French baguettes in Sweden
France is the only country so far to criminalize personal possession of nicotine pouches
